rules.statement: Maximum 1 B&W image per gallery. Place it mid-sequence as a palette cleanser. Never at the start or end.
rules.notes: Confirmed by every session. Never violated. Apply without hesitation.

rules.statement: If two images communicate the same idea at thumbnail size, only one survives — regardless of which is technically better.
rules.notes: Core deduplication rule. Confirmed across 5 sessions including first wedding (320 source images). Apply without hesitation.

rules.statement: Only the strongest lift/carry shot per distinct location survives. Exception: if two locations are visually so different that the same concept reads differently, one per location is acceptable.
rules.notes: Established at Alex+Aziz. Needs more sessions to confirm.

rules.statement: Walking shots must differ in direction (toward camera / across frame / away) and energy (confident / playful / editorial). Same orientation + same backdrop = same image — cut it.
rules.notes: Confirmed across 6 sessions. Never violated. Apply without hesitation.

rules.statement: Transit/streetcar shots must always be color. The vehicle color is the composition — a B&W version loses the entire point of the shot.
rules.notes: Confirmed at Alex+Aziz (TTC streetcar), Meghna+Puneeth (streetcar motion blur at night), and Mania+Hitesh (streetcar bench bokeh).

rules.statement: Open with a wide establishing shot that shows the scale of the location. Close with a wide architectural shot that echoes the opener. This creates a sense of journey.
rules.notes: Strong pattern from Alex+Aziz (DSCF5784 opener, DSCF6104 closer). Needs more sessions.

rules.statement: Motion blur is only selectable when it is clearly intentional (slow shutter, editorial feel) and reads as creative at thumbnail size. Accidental blur from camera shake is always a cut.
rules.notes: Basic quality threshold. Likely to confirm rapidly across sessions.

rules.statement: Maximum 2 images per gallery where a ring is a prominent focal point. One showcase (ring is the subject), one where it's secondary to another concept (DOF, motion, hands).
rules.notes: Created because AI proposed 4 ring shots; photographer cut 2. Rule confirmed and violated in the same session (AI violated it, photographer corrected it). Weight is 0 until next session confirms.

rules.statement: Ring-against-sky sequences of 4+ near-identical frames are common. Only ONE survives — the frame with the best light on the ring and cleanest sky background.
rules.notes: Created from Shuba+Rob. Very specific scenario but common in outdoor pre-wedding sessions.

rules.statement: Piggyback or carry shots from the same angle all read identically at thumbnail size. Only ONE survives regardless of how many frames were shot.
rules.notes: Created from Shuba+Rob photographer cut.

rules.statement: For casual/lifestyle pre-wedding sessions (not editorial), the cover image should be the most playful, joyful moment — not the most technically complex composition.
rules.notes: Contrasts with editorial sessions where layered compositions win.

rules.statement: Source sets under 30 images warrant a higher selection rate (up to 50-70%). Aggressive 10-15% culling at this scale leaves the gallery too thin.
rules.notes: Meghna+Puneeth (26 source images → 18 selected, 69%). Small sets need different logic.

rules.statement: Day-to-night sessions have a built-in narrative arc. Use it: sequence light-to-dark as a visual journey. Don't mix time-of-day shots — let the light tell the story.
rules.notes: Specific to sessions that span multiple lighting conditions.

rules.statement: When transitioning to a new location in the gallery sequence, the first image at that location should be a wide establishing shot — not a tight portrait.
rules.notes: Confirmed across 4 sessions including wedding. Now high confidence.

rules.statement: For sessions featuring formal gowns: maximum ONE fanned-out/editorial gown shot plus ONE in-motion gown shot per location. More than this reads as over-indexing on the outfit.
rules.notes: Specific to sessions with formal gowns. Needs more sessions to confirm.

rules.statement: Multi-outfit sessions should distribute selected images roughly evenly across outfits. Don't let the most photogenic outfit dominate the gallery.
rules.notes: Confirmed across 4 sessions. Swati+Geevan: 5 navy/UofT + 4 pink/island. Now high confidence.

rules.statement: If a proposal moment was captured in the session, it always makes the gallery — regardless of technical quality. This is an irreplaceable moment.
rules.notes: Same logic as ceremony moments in wedding day sessions.

rules.statement: Maximum 2-3 solo portraits (one subject alone) per gallery. Each must serve a distinct purpose — not interchangeable.
rules.notes: Solo groom walking sequences max 1 is a specific application of this rule.

rules.statement: Desaturated/color-pop edits (background desaturated, outfit color retained) should never be selected for the portfolio gallery. Always select the full-color version instead.
rules.notes: These edits feel gimmicky at portfolio scale. Full-color version is always preferred.

rules.statement: Spinning/twirl sequences of 4+ frames: only the single peak frame survives. The peak is where the skirt reaches maximum fan and the subject's face is visible.
rules.notes: Winter Special (7+ frames) and Mania+Hitesh (dance spin on lakefront concrete). Now medium confidence.

rules.statement: When outdoor backdrops are visually similar (e.g. all snow, all golden hour), interior shots become critical palette cleansers and should be prioritized to break monotony.
rules.notes: Winter sessions are the primary use case. May also apply to fog, overcast, or uniform lighting conditions.

rules.statement: When a session is published anonymously, use a descriptive album name (e.g. 'Winter Special', 'Spring Harbour') rather than couple initials or placeholder text.
rules.notes: Portfolio entry and all references should use the descriptive name consistently.

rules.statement: Shooting through foreground elements (foliage, branches, architectural features) is an intentional editorial technique that creates depth and intimacy. Never penalize these as 'obstructed' or 'obscured'. Evaluate them as layered compositions — the foreground IS the composition.
rules.notes: DSCF7608 was cut as 'too much leaf obscuring faces' when the foliage framing was the entire creative intent. Photographer flagged this as a clear selection miss.

rules.statement: Copy variants (-Copy1, -Copy2) that have a different crop, framing, or composition from the original are NOT the same image. Evaluate them independently. Only treat them as true variants when the composition is identical and only toning/exposure differs.
rules.notes: DSCF7547-Copy1 (full colonnade walk-away, bride looking back) was cut in favor of DSCF7547 (tight crop of just legs/dress). They share a filename but are completely different compositions. The Copy1 was the clear winner.

rules.statement: A portrait with layered foreground interest (plants, flowers, architectural elements framing the couple) is compositionally distinct from a clean portrait at the same location. Same location does NOT mean same image when the framing technique differs.
rules.notes: DSCF7631 (tropical plant foreground + City Hall architecture) was cut as 'identical to 7639' when it was a completely different composition using foreground layering. Photographer flagged this as a clear miss.

rules.statement: The final selection must draw from the entire album. Divide candidates into thirds by filename order (early/mid/late) and ensure each third contributes at least 20% of the final gallery. If a third is underrepresented, pull its top candidates even if it means dropping a slightly higher-scoring image from an over-represented third.
rules.notes: Anushka+Anthony: 14 of 20 selections came from the first third of 174 images. The entire outdoor portrait/editorial section (DSCF7400-7656) was nearly ignored. Photographer flagged three late-album images as obvious misses. Starting at high confidence because this is a structural flaw, not a subjective preference.

rules.statement: Persian/sofreh-aghd ceremonies must preserve: sugar-grinding ritual, Quran/altar wide, and signing-table close-up as three distinct ceremony slots, not one collapsed "ceremony" concept.
rules.notes: Identified in nora-ali: scanner flagged sofreh/Quran/sugar-grinding/signing as separate moments but concept field collapsed them to "ceremony". Future Persian/Iranian weddings should use finer concept enum.
